title: DynamoDB Single-Table Design
content:
DynamoDB Single-Table Design and DynamoDB Streams CDC In a multi-tenant SaaS platform, database design directly affects infrastructure cost, query performance, scalability, and tenant data isolation.
In this module, you will explore how the Smart Attendance SaaS Platform stores multiple entity types in a single Amazon DynamoDB table by using the Single-Table Design pattern.
You will also examine how DynamoDB Streams and Amazon EventBridge Pipes implement Change Data Capture (CDC) for asynchronous event processing.
1. Single-Table Schema Design Instead of creating separate DynamoDB tables for users, attendance records, tenants, and subscriptions, the system stores all entities in one table named:
smart-attendance-database The table uses a composite primary key consisting of:
Partition Key: PK Sort Key: SK The PK groups related records by tenant, while the SK identifies the entity type and individual record.
The proposed data model is shown below:
Entity Type Partition Key (PK) Sort Key (SK) Main Attributes Tenant Record TENANT#<tenantId> METADATA tenantName, plan, status, createdAt User Profile TENANT#<tenantId> USER#<userId> email, fullName, role, department Attendance Log TENANT#<tenantId> ATTENDANCE#<userId>#<timestamp> checkInTime, checkOutTime, status, location Subscription Billing TENANT#<tenantId> SUB#<billingId> planTier, amount, paymentStatus, expiryDate Example records:
PK: TENANT#company-001 SK: METADATA PK: TENANT#company-001 SK: USER#user-1001 PK: TENANT#company-001 SK: ATTENDANCE#user-1001#2026-07-22T08:00:00Z PK: TENANT#company-001 SK: SUB#invoice-2026-07 This structure allows the application to retrieve all data belonging to one organization through a single partition key.
2. Tenant Data Isolation Tenant isolation is one of the most important requirements in a multi-tenant SaaS application.
Every database request must use the tenant identifier extracted from the authenticated user’s JWT token.
The tenant partition key follows this format:
TENANT#<tenantId> For example:
TENANT#company-001 A Lambda function must never accept the tenant identifier directly from an untrusted request body without validation.
Instead, the tenant ID should be obtained from the authenticated JWT claims:
const claims = event.requestContext.authorizer.jwt.claims; const tenantId = claims["custom:tenantId"]; const partitionKey = `TENANT#${tenantId}`; A DynamoDB query can then be scoped to the authenticated tenant:
const command = new QueryCommand({ TableName: process.env.TABLE_NAME, KeyConditionExpression: "PK = :pk", ExpressionAttributeValues: { ":pk": `TENANT#${tenantId}` } }); This approach ensures that:
Users can only access records belonging to their own organization. Cross-tenant queries are prevented at the application layer. Authorization rules remain consistent across all Lambda functions. The risk of tenant data leakage is significantly reduced. 3. Review the DynamoDB Table in AWS Console Open the Amazon DynamoDB Console:
AWS Management Console → Amazon DynamoDB → Tables The deployed infrastructure creates a DynamoDB table named:
smart-attendance-database Verify that the table appears in the table list and has the following configuration:
Table status: Active Partition key: PK Sort key: SK Read capacity mode: On-Demand Write capacity mode: On-Demand Select smart-attendance-database to open its configuration page.
General Table Configuration In the Settings tab, review the general information of the DynamoDB table.
Confirm the following values:
Partition key: PK Sort key: SK Capacity mode: On-Demand Table status: Active The combination of PK and SK uniquely identifies each item stored in the table.
The On-Demand capacity mode automatically adjusts read and write throughput according to application traffic without requiring manual capacity provisioning.
This capacity mode is suitable for the Smart Attendance SaaS Platform because:
No initial capacity planning is required. Workload volume may vary across different tenants. The system only pays for actual read and write requests. It supports unpredictable check-in and check-out traffic. It simplifies database administration during development and demonstration. Point-in-Time Recovery Open the Backups tab and verify that Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) is enabled.
Expected status:
Point-in-Time Recovery: On PITR continuously protects the DynamoDB table and allows administrators to restore it to a selected point within the configured recovery period.
This feature helps protect the system from:
Accidental deletion of attendance records. Incorrect updates from application code. Data corruption. Operational mistakes. Unexpected deployment errors. The console also displays information such as:
Backup recovery period. Earliest available restore point. Latest available restore point. Existing system backups. Backup status and creation time. DynamoDB Backup Status Review the backup list displayed below the PITR configuration.
A backup with the status shown below confirms that DynamoDB data protection is active:
Status: Available The backup can be used to restore the table when data is accidentally modified or deleted.
Note: The third and fourth screenshots currently show nearly identical DynamoDB Backup pages. You may use one image for the PITR configuration and the other for the backup list, or replace the fourth screenshot with a DynamoDB Streams screenshot later.
Encryption The DynamoDB table is encrypted using an AWS KMS key.
The AWS SAM template defines the encryption key as:
DataKMSKey This configuration protects data at rest and allows administrators to manage permissions through AWS IAM and AWS KMS key policies.
Expected encryption configuration:
Encryption type: AWS KMS Key type: Customer Managed Key DynamoDB Streams Open the Exports and streams tab and verify that DynamoDB Streams is enabled with:
NEW_AND_OLD_IMAGES This configuration records both the previous version and the updated version of every modified item.
It allows downstream services to determine:
Which item was created. Which item was updated or deleted. Which attributes changed. What the previous values were. What the new values are. DynamoDB Streams provides the Change Data Capture source used by Amazon EventBridge Pipes and other asynchronous processing services.

title: Build Asynchronous Reporting Workflow
content:
Build Asynchronous Reporting Workflow In large-scale enterprise systems, generating monthly attendance reports for thousands of employees can take anywhere from 10 to 60 seconds. Processing these tasks synchronously through Amazon API Gateway may exceed the 30-second HTTP timeout, resulting in poor user experience and blocked requests.
In this module, you will build an asynchronous reporting workflow using AWS Step Functions, Amazon SQS, Amazon S3, and Amazon SES. This architecture allows report generation to run in the background while users receive an immediate response from the application.
1. Asynchronous Workflow Architecture The overall workflow is illustrated below:
Administrator │ ▼ Amazon API Gateway │ ▼ Amazon SQS Report Queue │ ▼ AWS Step Functions │ ▼ Amazon S3 │ ▼ Lambda Email Worker │ ▼ Amazon SES When an administrator clicks Export Monthly Report from the React application:
The frontend sends an HTTP POST request to Amazon API Gateway. API Gateway places the request into Amazon SQS Report Queue. The system immediately returns an HTTP 202 Accepted response, allowing the user interface to remain responsive. AWS Step Functions retrieves the queued request and coordinates the entire report generation workflow. After the report is successfully created, it is uploaded to Amazon S3 and a download link is emailed to the administrator through Amazon SES. 2. Report Generation Workflow AWS Step Functions orchestrates the complete report generation process.
The workflow consists of the following stages:
ValidateRequest
Validate input parameters such as tenantId and yearMonth. Ensure the request is valid before processing. InitializeReportStatus
Create or update a report record in Amazon DynamoDB. Set the report status to PROCESSING. GenerateReportFile
Invoke AWS Lambda. Retrieve attendance records from Amazon DynamoDB. Generate Excel or PDF attendance reports. WriteFileToS3
Upload the generated report to Amazon S3. Encrypt report files using AWS KMS. Store reports securely with lifecycle management if required. FinalizeReportStatus
Update the report status to COMPLETED. Save the generated S3 Object Key into DynamoDB. 3. AWS Step Functions Definition The Express State Machine is defined inside template.yaml.
Example configuration:
ReportStateMachine: Type: AWS::Serverless::StateMachine Properties: Type: EXPRESS Role: !GetAtt ReportWorkflowRole.Arn Using an Express Workflow provides several advantages:
Fast execution. Lower operational cost. Automatic scaling. Suitable for high-volume reporting workloads. 4. Configure Amazon SQS Amazon SQS acts as a buffering layer between the API and the backend processing services.
Example resource:
EmailQueue: Type: AWS::SQS::Queue Amazon SQS provides several benefits:
Decouples frontend requests from backend processing. Improves application responsiveness. Buffers workload spikes. Supports automatic retry for failed processing. 5. Configure Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) To improve system reliability, failed messages are automatically redirected to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) after multiple unsuccessful processing attempts.
Example resource:
EmailDLQ: Type: AWS::SQS::Queue Redrive Policy:
RedrivePolicy: deadLetterTargetArn: !GetAtt EmailDLQ.Arn maxReceiveCount: 3 If the Lambda Email Worker fails to process the same message three consecutive times, Amazon SQS automatically moves the message to the Dead Letter Queue.
This approach helps to:
Prevent message loss. Simplify troubleshooting. Support message replay after issues are resolved. 6. Store Reports in Amazon S3 Once the report has been successfully generated:
Upload the report to Amazon S3. Encrypt the object using AWS KMS. Apply Lifecycle Rules or Intelligent-Tiering to optimize storage costs. Example report location:
reports/monthly-attendance-report.xlsx The generated S3 Object Key is then stored in Amazon DynamoDB for future retrieval.
7. Send Notification Emails with Amazon SES After report generation is completed:
Amazon EventBridge publishes a ReportGenerated event. The Lambda Email Worker retrieves messages from Amazon SQS. Amazon SES sends an email notification to the administrator. The email includes:
Report generation status. Report creation timestamp. Secure download link stored in Amazon S3. 8. Complete Processing Flow Administrator │ ▼ React Web Application │ ▼ Amazon API Gateway │ ▼ Amazon SQS Report Queue │ ▼ AWS Step Functions │ ├── Validate Request ├── Update Report Status ├── Generate Report ├── Upload Report to Amazon S3 └── Update DynamoDB │ ▼ Amazon EventBridge │ ▼ Amazon SQS Email Queue │ ▼ Lambda Email Worker │ ▼ Amazon SES │ ▼ Administrator Module Summary After completing this module, you have successfully:
Built an asynchronous reporting workflow using AWS Step Functions. Buffered report generation requests with Amazon SQS. Stored generated reports securely in Amazon S3. Automated email notifications using Amazon SES. Implemented a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) to improve fault tolerance. Learned how an Event-Driven Architecture enables scalable and reliable processing without blocking user requests. The reporting workflow is now ready to integrate with the React frontend, providing administrators with a responsive and reliable monthly attendance reporting system.

content:

title: Deploy Frontend & CloudFront CDN
content:
Deploy React SPA Frontend and CloudFront CDN In this module, you will build the React Single Page Application (SPA), upload the production build artifacts to Amazon S3, and distribute the application globally through Amazon CloudFront.
The frontend architecture also uses a Custom Origin Verification Header named x-origin-verify to prevent users from bypassing CloudFront and directly accessing the Amazon API Gateway endpoint.
1. Edge Protection Architecture The frontend request flow is designed as follows:
User Browser │ ▼ Amazon Route 53 │ ▼ Amazon CloudFront CDN AWS WAF + AWS Shield │ ├──────────────► Amazon S3 │ React Static Assets │ └──────────────► Amazon API Gateway Header: x-origin-verify The main components include:
Amazon S3 SPA Bucket stores the React static assets, including index.html, JavaScript, CSS, fonts, and image files. Amazon CloudFront distributes the frontend application through global edge locations to reduce latency. AWS WAF protects the CloudFront distribution against common web attacks and suspicious requests. AWS Shield Standard provides basic protection against distributed denial-of-service attacks. Custom Verification Header allows CloudFront to attach a secret value to requests forwarded to Amazon API Gateway. Amazon API Gateway rejects requests that do not contain the expected x-origin-verify header. Direct public access to the S3 bucket should remain disabled. Users access the frontend through CloudFront instead of requesting objects directly from Amazon S3.
2. Configure Frontend Environment Variables Navigate to the frontend directory:
cd ../frontend Create a production environment file:
.env.production Add the backend and authentication values obtained from the AWS SAM deployment outputs:
VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://xxxxxxx.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod VITE_COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID=ap-southeast-1_xxxxxxxxx VITE_COGNITO_CLIENT_ID=xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx VITE_AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-1 The environment variables are used for:
Connecting the React application to Amazon API Gateway. Identifying the Amazon Cognito User Pool. Identifying the Cognito App Client. Configuring the AWS Region used by the application. Do not place AWS Access Keys or Secret Access Keys inside frontend environment files. Frontend code is delivered to user browsers and must never contain permanent AWS credentials.
3. Install Frontend Dependencies Install the required npm packages:
npm install After installation, verify that the node_modules directory and the package lock file are available.
You can start the application locally for testing:
npm run dev The development server is typically available at:
http://localhost:5173 Verify the following functions before building:
Login with Amazon Cognito. Access protected application pages. Call backend APIs through Amazon API Gateway. Display attendance information. Submit check-in and check-out requests. Request monthly report generation. 4. Build the React Application Compile the production application:
npm run build After a successful build, the dist/ directory is generated:
dist/ ├── assets/ │ ├── index-xxxx.js │ └── index-xxxx.css ├── favicon.ico └── index.html The generated files are optimized for production deployment.
The build process usually performs:
JavaScript and CSS minification. Asset optimization. Module bundling. Environment variable replacement. Production dependency optimization. 5. Create the Frontend S3 Bucket Retrieve the current AWS Account ID:
aws sts get-caller-identity \ --query Account \ --output text Create a globally unique bucket name using the Account ID:
aws s3 mb \ s3://smart-attendance-spa-hosting-<AWS_ACCOUNT_ID> \ --region ap-southeast-1 Example:
aws s3 mb \ s3://smart-attendance-spa-hosting-123456789012 \ --region ap-southeast-1 Keep S3 Block Public Access enabled when the bucket is used behind Amazon CloudFront.
6. Upload the React Build to Amazon S3 Synchronize the local dist/ directory with the S3 bucket:
aws s3 sync \ dist/ \ s3://smart-attendance-spa-hosting-<AWS_ACCOUNT_ID> \ --delete The --delete option removes old S3 objects that no longer exist in the local build directory.
To verify the uploaded files:
aws s3 ls \ s3://smart-attendance-spa-hosting-<AWS_ACCOUNT_ID> \ --recursive Expected objects include:
index.html favicon.svg icons.svg assets/index-xxxx.js assets/index-xxxx.css The following terminal output confirms that the React application was successfully built with Vite and synchronized to the Amazon S3 hosting bucket:
The successful output confirms that:
Vite completed the production build. The optimized files were generated inside the dist/ directory. The JavaScript and CSS bundles were uploaded to Amazon S3. The previous S3 objects not present in the latest build were removed through the --delete option. The frontend assets are ready to be served through Amazon CloudFront or the Amazon S3 website endpoint. 7. Configure Amazon CloudFront Open the Amazon CloudFront Console and create a new distribution.
Configure the S3 bucket as the frontend origin.
Recommended settings:
Origin domain: Smart Attendance S3 Bucket. Origin access: Origin Access Control. Viewer protocol policy: Redirect HTTP to HTTPS. Allowed HTTP methods: GET and HEAD. Compress objects automatically: Enabled. Default root object: index.html. Create an additional origin for Amazon API Gateway if API requests are routed through the same CloudFront distribution.
For the API Gateway origin, configure the custom header:
Header name: x-origin-verify Header value: SaaS-Secure-Verification-Token-2026 The header value must match the secret configured in the backend deployment.
8. Protect Amazon API Gateway with the Verification Header The backend should validate the custom header before processing protected API requests.
Example Lambda validation:
const expectedSecret = process.env.ORIGIN_VERIFY_SECRET; const receivedSecret = event.headers?.["x-origin-verify"] || event.headers?.["X-Origin-Verify"]; if (receivedSecret !== expectedSecret) { return { statusCode: 403, body: JSON.stringify({ message: "Forbidden" }) }; } This configuration helps prevent direct access to the API Gateway URL.
The expected request path becomes:
User → CloudFront → x-origin-verify header → API Gateway → AWS Lambda A direct request that does not pass through CloudFront should receive:
HTTP 403 Forbidden 9. Configure SPA Error Handling React applications use client-side routing. When users refresh a route such as:
/dashboard Amazon CloudFront may request /dashboard as a physical object and receive a 403 or 404 response from S3.
Configure CloudFront custom error responses:
HTTP Error Code: 403 Response Page Path: /index.html HTTP Response Code: 200 HTTP Error Code: 404 Response Page Path: /index.html HTTP Response Code: 200 This allows React Router to handle application routes correctly.
10. CloudFront Account Verification Issue New AWS accounts may receive an error similar to:
Your account must be verified before you can add new CloudFront resources This restriction may prevent immediate CloudFront distribution creation.
As a temporary workaround, enable Amazon S3 Static Website Hosting:
aws s3 website \ s3://smart-attendance-spa-hosting-<AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>/ \ --index-document index.html \ --error-document index.html The temporary website URL follows this format:
http://smart-attendance-spa-hosting-<AWS_ACCOUNT_ID>.s3-website-ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com For the production architecture, request CloudFront access through the AWS Support Center under account verification.
11. Invalidate the CloudFront Cache After uploading a new frontend version, create a CloudFront invalidation so users receive the latest assets immediately:
aws cloudfront create-invalidation \ --distribution-id <DISTRIBUTION_ID> \ --paths "/*" Verify the invalidation status:
aws cloudfront get-invalidation \ --distribution-id <DISTRIBUTION_ID> \ --id <INVALIDATION_ID> Wait until the status becomes:
Completed 12. Verify the Deployed Application Open the CloudFront domain in a web browser:
https://dxxxxxxxxxxxxx.cloudfront.net Verify the following functions:
The React SPA loads successfully. Static assets are delivered through CloudFront. HTTP requests are redirected to HTTPS. Login works with Amazon Cognito. API requests reach Amazon API Gateway. Direct API Gateway requests without x-origin-verify are rejected. Check-in and check-out functions work correctly. Attendance history is displayed. Monthly report requests return an accepted response. Browser refresh works on nested React routes. Module Summary After completing this module, you have:
Configured the production environment variables for the React application. Installed frontend dependencies. Built the optimized React SPA. Created an Amazon S3 bucket for frontend assets. Uploaded the application using AWS CLI. Configured Amazon CloudFront for global content delivery. Protected the API origin using x-origin-verify. Configured client-side route fallback. Invalidated the CloudFront cache after deployment. Verified the frontend and backend integration. The Smart Attendance SaaS Platform is now available through a globally distributed and secure web frontend.

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship at Amazon Web Services Vietnam Co., Ltd. from May 5, 2026 to July 30, 2026, I had the opportunity to learn, practice, and apply the knowledge acquired at university in a real-world working environment.
Throughout the internship, I participated in designing AWS Serverless solution architectures, writing technical documentation, publishing AWS technical blogs, attending community events and workshops, and developing a demonstration project based on AWS Cloud technologies. These activities helped me improve both my technical knowledge and soft skills, including cloud architecture design, programming, system analysis, technical writing, presentation, teamwork, and communication.
Regarding work attitude, I always tried to complete assigned tasks responsibly, complied with company regulations, proactively learned new technologies, and actively collaborated with mentors and teammates to improve work quality and productivity.
To objectively evaluate my internship performance, I assessed myself based on the following criteria:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding cloud technologies, applying AWS knowledge in practice, using development tools, and producing quality work ☐ ✅ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Ability to quickly absorb new knowledge and continuously learn new AWS services ☐ ✅ ☐ 3 Proactiveness & Initiative Taking initiative in learning, solving problems, and completing assigned tasks without constant supervision ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing assigned tasks on schedule while maintaining quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Discipline Following company regulations, working hours, and established development processes ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Desire to improve Willingness to receive feedback and continuously improve technical and personal skills ☐ ✅ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas clearly, reporting progress, and communicating effectively with mentors and teammates ☐ ✅ ☐ 8 Teamwork Collaborating effectively with team members and supporting common project objectives ✅ ☐ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Demonstrating professionalism, respecting colleagues, and maintaining a positive working attitude ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving mindset Identifying problems, analyzing root causes, and proposing practical solutions ✅ ☐ ☐ 11 Project contribution Contributing technical ideas, completing assigned work, and supporting the overall project ✅ ☐ ☐ 12 Overall Overall evaluation of the internship experience ✅ ☐ ☐ Areas for Improvement Based on the evaluation above, I have identified several areas that require further improvement throughout my professional development:
Professional Knowledge & Technical Skills: Continue expanding my knowledge of advanced AWS Serverless architecture, cloud security, DevOps practices, and AWS cost optimization techniques to better support enterprise-scale systems.
Discipline & Professional Workflow: Improve personal discipline by following working schedules more consistently, organizing tasks efficiently, and adhering to professional software development processes.
Learning Agility: Increase the speed of learning emerging cloud technologies and actively seek feedback from mentors to continuously improve technical competence.
Communication Skills: Enhance both technical and interpersonal communication skills, especially when presenting technical solutions, writing documentation, and collaborating within development teams.
Overall, this internship provided valuable practical experience and strengthened my understanding of cloud computing, AWS Serverless technologies, teamwork, and professional software development practices. It also motivated me to continue improving my technical abilities and prepare for a future career as a Cloud Engineer or Solutions Architect.

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
This section presents my personal reflections, evaluations, and suggestions after completing the internship at First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ). I hope these comments can contribute to improving the program for future internship cohorts.
Overall Evaluation 1. Internship Environment The learning environment at First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) is open, dynamic, and encourages self-learning. Throughout the internship, I had the opportunity to explore a wide range of AWS services and work on practical cloud-related tasks rather than simply following theoretical tutorials.
The community communication channels were always active, making it easy for interns to exchange ideas, discuss technical issues, and support one another during labs and project implementation. This collaborative atmosphere greatly motivated me to become more proactive in learning and problem-solving.
2. Support from Mentors and Admin Team The mentors played the role of guides instead of simply providing answers. Whenever I encountered problems related to AWS services or system deployment, they encouraged me to analyze the root cause, investigate CloudWatch logs, and gradually identify appropriate solutions on my own.
In addition, the admin team continuously updated important announcements and organized Community Day events, workshops, and technical sharing sessions. These activities provided valuable opportunities to learn from experienced engineers and industry professionals.
3. Relevance to My Academic Major The internship program was highly relevant to my university major, especially in the areas of Cloud Computing, AWS Serverless Architecture, and DevOps.
By designing the architecture of the Smart Attendance SaaS Platform, writing technical blogs, developing workshop documentation, and studying AWS services, I was able to apply theoretical knowledge from university to practical cloud engineering scenarios while gaining a deeper understanding of modern software deployment processes.
4. Learning Opportunities and Skill Development This was the aspect I appreciated the most during the internship.
Besides learning numerous AWS services, I also strengthened many important professional skills, including:
Designing cloud architectures on AWS. Writing technical documentation and workshop guides. Creating technical blog posts. Presenting ideas and reporting work progress. Collaborating effectively within a team. Researching official AWS documentation to solve technical problems independently. Participating in Community Day events also broadened my understanding of current technology trends, Artificial Intelligence, Cloud Computing, and career development within the IT industry.
5. Community Culture One of the strongest aspects of FCAJ is its knowledge-sharing culture.
Members were always willing to help each other whenever someone encountered deployment issues or difficulties while completing AWS labs. This supportive environment made me more confident in asking questions, sharing ideas, and learning collaboratively with other interns.
6. Learning Resources and Support The program provided comprehensive learning materials, technical workshops, and AWS Sandbox environments that allowed interns to practice without worrying about cloud service costs.
This gave me the opportunity to gain hands-on experience with many AWS services such as Amazon EC2, Amazon S3, Amazon CloudFront, Amazon API Gateway, AWS Lambda, Amazon DynamoDB, Amazon Cognito, and many other cloud services in an environment similar to real-world production systems.
Survey Responses What I Was Most Satisfied With The most valuable part of the internship was having direct access to AWS services through hands-on labs, workshops, and practical assignments. Designing cloud architectures, studying AWS services, and writing technical documentation significantly improved both my technical knowledge and my confidence in working with cloud technologies.
Areas for Improvement I suggest adding individual One-on-One Review sessions during the middle of the internship. These meetings would allow mentors to evaluate each intern’s progress, identify strengths and weaknesses, and provide personalized guidance before the final project stage.
Would I Recommend This Program? Yes.
I would definitely recommend the First Cloud AI Journey internship program to students who are interested in Cloud Computing, AWS, or DevOps. The program provides valuable practical experience, a supportive learning community, and a solid foundation for future careers in cloud engineering.
Suggestions and Future Expectations To further improve the internship program, I would like to suggest the following:
AWS Certification Roadmap: Provide learning resources or mentoring sessions for internationally recognized AWS certifications such as AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner and AWS Certified Solutions Architect – Associate, helping students prepare for professional certifications after completing the internship.
Cross-Team Technical Sharing Sessions: Organize short technical presentations where different project teams introduce their solution architectures and design decisions. This would allow interns to learn multiple approaches for solving similar cloud engineering problems.
Advanced Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Content: Besides AWS SAM, include additional workshops or reference materials covering Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, and AWS CDK to better prepare students for modern DevOps and Cloud Engineering roles.
Overall, the internship at First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) provided me with valuable technical knowledge, practical experience, and many opportunities for personal and professional growth. It strengthened my understanding of Cloud Computing, enhanced my technical skills, and established a solid foundation for pursuing a future career in AWS Cloud Engineering.
